summary: I was born in New Camp 6, Bylkuppe, a Tibetan refugee camp located in the southern plains of India on March 23, 1990. Growing up there, I was surrounded by rice paddies, monsoons, and the flavors of spicy curries, which shaped my early experiences. Although it was my birthplace, Bylkuppe was a temporary home, as my parents always reminded me that we were guests in India. The camp was a place where Tibetan culture and hope for return were preserved among exiles like my family.

Tibet has always been a distant and almost magical place for me, described by elder Tibetans as a perfect land before the Communist Chinese invasion. Though I never set foot there, my parents and community instilled in me the identity of being Tibetan and the hope that one day Tibet would be free again. I yearned to breathe its thin air and reconnect with my ancestral homeland, but it remained surreal and far away throughout my childhood. Tibet represents my true roots and the homeland I long to return to.

India was the country where I grew up as a Tibetan refugee, specifically in the camp of Bylkuppe. My family lived there as guests, and although I experienced the culture through food and environment, I never felt it was my true home. India was a place of exile, where my parents reminded me that our stay was temporary until Tibet could be free. It was in India that my early identity was shaped amidst the rice paddies and monsoon seasons.

When I was seven, my family immigrated to the United States, which offered us opportunities that India could not due to our refugee status. Moving from rural India to the skyscraper city of Chicago was a huge change; America was clean, orderly, and cold. I struggled to feel a sense of belonging despite learning English and adopting American customs. Later, I served honorably in the United States Marine Corps to deepen my connection to this country that welcomed me, and after my service, I pursued education at the University of Minnesota to build a future here.

Chicago was my first home in the United States after leaving India when I was seven years old. The city was a stark contrast to the rice paddies of Bylkuppe, with its towering skyscrapers and cold weather. Growing up there, I adapted by picking up basketball and an American accent, though I still struggled with feeling truly American. Chicago marked the beginning of my journey to find a place where I could belong in this new country.

After high school, I enlisted in the United States Marine Corps to serve the country that had welcomed me as a refugee and to find a sense of belonging. Serving honorably for four years, I was challenged physically and mentally, traveled the world, and made a difference through my service. The Marines gave me a strong sense of identity and discipline, and though I later moved on, I deeply miss the ethos and camaraderie of the Corps.

After completing my service in the Marines, I enrolled at the University of Minnesota to broaden my horizons and prepare for my future. The university became a place where I could learn about the world and my own origins, helping me to diversify my life beyond the military. It was an important step in my journey to find a home and understand my identity as a global citizen.

During my time in the Marine Corps, I lived in various places, including Okinawa, Japan. Okinawa was part of my experience traveling and serving abroad, which broadened my perspective on the world. Living there, along with other locations like Japan and aboard a naval boat, contributed to my understanding that home can be many places, even if my true homeland remains Tibet.

Japan was one of the countries I lived in while serving in the United States Marine Corps, including time spent in Okinawa. My experiences there were part of a larger journey that took me away from my refugee camp origins to a global perspective. Living in Japan helped me appreciate different cultures and reinforced my belief that home is not just one place but can be found across the world.
